coredata.dc:title: The visible light-driven and self-powered photoelectrochemical biosensor for organophosphate pesticides detection based on nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots for the signal amplification
coredata.dc:creator: Cheng, Wenjie
Zheng, Zengyao
Yang, Jianying
Chen, Min
Yao, Qingwei
Chen, Yaowen
Gao, Wenhua
coredata.dc:description: A novel visible light driven self-powdered photoelectrochemical (PEC) biosensor was constructed for organophosphorus pesticides (OPs). The biosensor was prepared by taking indium tin oxide (ITO) as the substrate, titanium dioxide (TiO2) nanoparticles as the photoactive substrate material, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) as the photosensitizer and acetylcholinesterase (AChE) as the biorecognition molecule. Because of the excellent optical performances of NCQDs, the photocurrent response of the NCQD/TiO2/ITO electrode were improved obviously compared with TiO2/ITO electrode. The AChE immobilized on the surface of the PEC biosensor can catalytic hydrolysis of acetylcholine chloride (ATCl) to generate thiocholine, which can serve as the electron donor to enhance the separation of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs, therefore, the photocurrent response of the PEC biosensor elevated greatly. The bioactivity of the AChE was inhibited by OPs where chlorpyrifos was used as the model, the production of the thiocholine was declined, leading to a reduction of the photocurrent response. The prepared PEC biosensor can achieve the sensitive detection of chlorpyrifos with the board detection range varied from 0.001 μg mL−1 to 1.5 μg mL−1 and the detection limit was about 0.07 ng mL−1 (S/N = 3). It is the first time that NCQDs/TiO2 is adopted to construct the PEC biosensor for chlorpyrifos detection. The obtained PEC biosensor can also achieve the detection of chlorpyrifos in the lake water and Chinese cabbage samples, suggesting that the fabricated PEC biosensor had potential application for the detection of OPs in environment and food.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Visible light absorption
High fluorescence quantum yield
Upconversion light
Organophosphorus pesticides
Photoelectrochemical detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation the NCQD/TiO2 modified ITO electrode
The UV–vis absorption spectra of CQDs and NCQDs
The PL spectrum of CQDs and NCQDs
The characterizations of TiO2 nanoparticles, NCQDs and NCQD/TiO2 nanocomposites
Reproducibility, stability and selectivity of PEC biosensor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Science and Technology Planning Project of Guangdong Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We are grateful for the financial support from the Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province (No. 2014A030313480 ) and the Science & Technology Project of Guangdong Province (No. 2013B030600001 & No. 2016A020223014 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–vis absorption spectra of (a) CQDs and (b) NCQDs, the inset shows the corresponding photographs of the as-prepared (a) CQDs and (b) NCQD suspension under room light (left) and upon irradiation with 365 nm UV light (right). PL spectra of (B) CQDs and (E) NCQDs, the normalized PL intensity of (C) CQDs and (F) NCQDs under different excitation wavelengths from 300 nm to 420 nm in 10 nm increments. The upconversion PL of (D) CQDs and (G) NCQDs with excitation wavelengths from 500 nm to 900 nm in 20 nm increments. (H) SEM images of TiO2. Inset, size distribution of TiO2. (I) TEM images of NCQDs. Inset, size distribution of NCQDs.

(A) AFM image of CQDs. (B) XRD patterns of (a) TiO2, (b) NCQDs and (c) NCQD/TiO2. (C) EDX spectrum and (D) elemental mapping images of NCQDs. (E) UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) and (F) the Plots of (αhν)1/2 versus photon energy (hν) of (a) TiO2 and (b) NCQD/TiO2. (G) LSV curves of the (a) TiO2/ITO and (b) NCQD/TiO2/ITO electrode under illumination (λ ≥ 420 nm), (c) NCQD/TiO2/ITO electrode under dark in 0.1 M Na2SO4 at the scan rate of 50 mV/s (H) OCP curves of (a) TiO2/ITO and (b) NCQD/TiO2/ITO electrode in 0.1 M PBS (pH = 7.4). (I) EIS of (a) TiO2/ITO, and (b) NCQD/TiO2/ITO under dark, (a') TiO2/ITO and (b') NCQD/TiO2/ITO under visible light (λ ≥ 420 nm).

(A) EIS spectra in 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− solution containing 0.1 M KCl of (a) bare ITO, (b) TiO2/ITO, (c) NCQD/TiO2/ITO, (d) CS/NCQD/TiO2/ITO, (e) GLD/CS/NCQD/TiO2/ITO, (f) AChE/GLD/CS/NCQD/TiO2/ITO, (g) BSA/AChE/GLD/CS/NCQDs/TiO2/ITO. (B) Photocurrent intensities of (a) bare ITO, (b) TiO2/ITO, (c) NCQD/TiO2/ITO, (d) CS/NCQD/TiO2/ITO, (e) GLD/CS/NCQD/TiO2/ITO, (f) AChE/GLD/CS/NCQD/TiO2/ITO, BSA/AChE/GLD/CS/NCQD/TiO2/ITO in PBS (0.1 M, pH = 7.4) without (g) and with (h) 0.8 mM ATCl under visible light (λ ≥ 420 nm) without impressed voltage.

The effects of (A) TiO2 concentration on the photocurrent intensities of TiO2/ITO electrodes in PBS (0.1 M, pH = 7.4), (B) immersion time of the TiO2/ITO electrodes in 0.3 mg mL−1 NCQD solution on the photocurrent intensities of the NCQD/TiO2/ITO electrodes in PBS (0.1 M, pH = 7.4), (C) the AChE concentration and (D) the incubation time of AChE on the photocurrent intensities of AChE/GLD/CS/NCQD/TiO2/ITO electrode in PBS (0.1 M, pH = 7.4), (E) ATCl concentration on the photocurrent intensities of the BSA/AChE/GLD/CS/NCQD/TiO2/ITO electrode in PBS (0.1 M, pH = 7.4), (F) solution pH of PBS (0.1 M) containing 0.8 mM ATCl on the photocurrent intensities of the BSA/AChE/GLD/CS/NCQD/TiO2/ITO electrodes and (G) the inhibition time in the presence of 0.08 μg mL−1 chlorpyrifos on the photocurrent intensities of the BSA/AChE/GLD/CS/NCQD/TiO2/ITO electrodes in PBS (0.1 M, pH = 7.4) containing 0.8 mM ATCl. All tests obtained under visible light (λ ≥ 420 nm) without impressed voltage. Every data point is the average value obtained from three independent measurements at the same conditions, and the standard deviation is indicated by the error bar.

(A) The photocurrent responses of the PEC biosensor at different concentrations of chlorpyrifos: (a–h) 0.001, 0.005, 0.01, 0.04, 0.08, 0.2, 1.0, 1.5 μg mL−1 and (B) The corresponding relation between the photocurrent signal of the PEC biosensor and the chlorpyrifos concentrations ranged from 0 to 1.5 μg mL−1 in PBS (0.1 M, pH = 7.4) containing 0.8 mM ATCl under visible light (λ ≥ 420 nm) without impressed voltage, inset is the linear relationship between the logarithm of chlorpyrifos concentrations and the photocurrent. (C) The photocurrent responses of seven separate as-constructed biosensor electrodes in PBS (0.1 M, pH = 7.4) containing 0.8 mM ATCl in the presence of 0.04 μg mL−1 chlorpyrifos under visible light (λ ≥ 420 nm) without impressed voltage. (D) The photocurrent responses of as-constructed biosensor electrodes after different storage time in PBS (0.1 M, pH = 7.4) containing 0.8 mM ATCl under visible light (λ ≥ 420 nm) without impressed voltage. (E) Selectivity of the PEC biosensor in PBS (0.1 M, pH = 7.4) containing 0.8 mM ATCl in the presence of 1 μg ml−1 chlorpyrifos, 1 μg ml−1 other pesticides (δ-Hexachlorocyclohexane, Fenvalerate, Deltamethrin, 2,2-Bis(4-chlorophenyl)-1,1-dichloroethylene (p, p’-DDE), 2,4′-Dichlorodiphenyltrichloroethane (o,p′-DDT)), and 100 μg ml−1 inorganic ion (K+, Na+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Cl−, NO3 −, SO4 2−). Every data point is the average value obtained from three independent measurements at the same conditions, and the standard deviation is indicated by the error bar.
The mechanism of the as-prepared PEC biosensor for chlorpyrifos detection.
Schematic illustration of the preparation process of the PEC biosensor for chlorpyrifos detection.
Recovery of PEC biosensor for chlorpyrifos detection.

head.ce:title: The visible light-driven and self-powered photoelectrochemical biosensor for organophosphate pesticides detection based on nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots for the signal amplification

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Laboratory for Preparation and Application of Ordered.Structural Materials of Guangdong Province, Shantou University, Shantou, Guangdong 515063, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Laboratory for Preparation and Application of Ordered.Structural Materials of Guangdong Province

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Detergents and Cosmetics Products Quality Supervision and Inspection Center (Guangdong), Shantou, Guangdong 515041, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Detergents and Cosmetics Products Quality Supervision and Inspection Center (Guangdong)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Analysis & Testing Center, Shantou University, Shantou, Guangdong 515063, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Chemistry, Shantou University, Shantou, Guangdong 515063, PR China..

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel visible light driven self-powdered photoelectrochemical (PEC) biosensor was constructed for organophosphorus pesticides (OPs). The biosensor was prepared by taking indium tin oxide (ITO) as the substrate, titanium dioxide (TiO2) nanoparticles as the photoactive substrate material, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) as the photosensitizer and acetylcholinesterase (AChE) as the biorecognition molecule. Because of the excellent optical performances of NCQDs, the photocurrent response of the NCQD/TiO2/ITO electrode were improved obviously compared with TiO2/ITO electrode. The AChE immobilized on the surface of the PEC biosensor can catalytic hydrolysis of acetylcholine chloride (ATCl) to generate thiocholine, which can serve as the electron donor to enhance the separation of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs, therefore, the photocurrent response of the PEC biosensor elevated greatly. The bioactivity of the AChE was inhibited by OPs where chlorpyrifos was used as the model, the production of the thiocholine was declined, leading to a reduction of the photocurrent response. The prepared PEC biosensor can achieve the sensitive detection of chlorpyrifos with the board detection range varied from 0.001 μg mL−1 to 1.5 μg mL−1 and the detection limit was about 0.07 ng mL−1 (S/N = 3). It is the first time that NCQDs/TiO2 is adopted to construct the PEC biosensor for chlorpyrifos detection. The obtained PEC biosensor can also achieve the detection of chlorpyrifos in the lake water and Chinese cabbage samples, suggesting that the fabricated PEC biosensor had potential application for the detection of OPs in environment and food.

A novel visible light driven self-powdered photoelectrochemical (PEC) biosensor was constructed by adopting the NCQDs/TiO2 as the photoactive matrix and AChE as the biological recognition molecule for the sensitive detection of organophosphorus pesticides (OPs) by using chlorpyrifos as the model.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: In recent decades, Organophosphorus pesticides (OPs), one kind of pesticides with highly toxic and high efficiency, have been extensively applied in agriculture all over the world to prevent the crops and vegetables from being attacked by insects and guarantee the farm production [1]. Nevertheless, with the development of agriculture, the high dosage use and improper disposal of OPs have given rise to some very serious problems, such as environmental pollution and OPs residues in agricultural products. Because the OPs can bind to acetylcholinesterase (AChE), it can lead to the serious and irreversible inhibition the activity of AChE and then disrupt the nervous system of the mammal or even result in death [2]. For the past few years, more than 100 thousand of people had died from OPs poisoning, because the food or drinking water which is contaminated with OPs has been taken in Ref. [3]. With hypertoxicity and fatalness, OPs have been prohibited in the agricultural application based on the provisions of the Food and Agricultural Organization (FAO) and the United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP) [4]. Nowadays, people are paying more and more attention to body health, environmental conservation, and food safety. So, a lot of detection methods have been developed for OPs monitoring [5,6], including liquid chromatography-massspectrometry (LC-MS) [7], (enzyme-based) electrochemical sensor [8], and fluorescence biosensor [9], and so on. All these strategies can achieve the selective and sensitive detection of OPs. However, all methods either need expensive laboratory equipment, complex sample preparation or require skillful experimenter. So, the eco-friendly, energy-saving, easy-to-operate and highly sensitive detection methods are eagerly needed to monitor the OPs.

Photoelectrochemical (PEC) detection, acted as the newly emerged detection method, have been apace developed [10]. Compared with conventional electrochemical and optical method, PEC biosensor has low background noise and high sensitivity owing to the completed separation between the detection signal (photocurrent) and excitation source (light). In addition, PEC biosensor also possesses many other advantages, such as simply instruments, easy operation and low cost [11]. So, PEC biosensors have been broadly applied to the detection of inorganic ions [12], DNA sequence [13], disease or cancer markers [14], and so on. Moreover, the self-powered PEC biosensors had been developed, in which no extra voltage was provided. Without extra voltage, on the hand, the self-powered PEC biosensor can make sufficient use of light, on the other hand, it also can avoid the interference of oxidation/reductive substances and reduces damage to biological molecules on the electrode to improve the sensitivity of the detection [15,16]. The photocurrent response of the self-powered PEC biosensor heavily depends on the photoactive material. Hence, photoactive materials play a very important role in the fabrication of the PEC biosensor. Until now, many photoactive materials have been applied in PEC biosensor, including ZnO [17], TiO2 [18], g-C3N4 [19], and so on. Especially, attributed to the high biological and chemical stability, abundant raw materials, good optical properties and nontoxicity, TiO2 have been regarded as the attractive candidate for applying in the preparation of the PEC biosensor. However, shared with the wide band gap, TiO2 can only absorb ultraviolet (UV) light (λ＜400 nm), that does not benefit to make the best use of the solar light, because the UV light only accounts for less than 5% in solar light [20]. And the application of TiO2 for biosensor also is limited, because the UV light can damage the biomolecules. So, many methods have been developed to enable TiO2 to effectively utilize the visible light, for intense, nonmetal atoms doping modification [21], noble metal modification [22], and dye sensitization [23].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are the newly emerged economic environmentally friendly carbon materials, which possesses small sizes below 10 nm and narrow band gap. Compared with semiconductor quantum dots, such as CdTe [24], CdS [25], CQDs are more environmentally friendly. Owing to its preeminent optical properties, size-dependent emission wavelength, quantum confinement, photostability, good water solubility and biocompatibility, CQDs have been applied in many fields, including solar cells [26], fluorescent sensors [27,28] etc., Meanwhile, CQDs have the upconversion photoluminescence (PL) properties that it can absorb long wavelength light with the emission of light at short wavelength, that is different from traditional inorganic semiconductor-based QDs [29]. In fact, CQDs still have some shortcoming, for example, low fluorescence quantum yield and poor electron transfer capability. Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) obtain enormous attention because nitrogen elements doping will cause charge delocalization and provide tunable optical property. The gained NCQDs possess high fluorescence quantum yield, enhanced electron transfer capability, and conductivity. So, NCQDs can be expected to exhibit superexcellence performances in the PEC biosensor.

Herein, the visible light-driven and self-powdered PEC biosensor was fabricated for the OPs detection. As depicted in Scheme 1 , in the process of construction, the TiO2 was served as the photoactive substrate material, hydrothermal prepared NCQDs was used as the photosensitizer for signal amplification. Owning to narrow band gap, high fluorescence quantum yield, fast electron transportation and upconversion photoluminescence properties of the NCQDs, the photocurrent response of the obtained NCQD/TiO2/ITO electrode showed about 42 times than that of the TiO2/ITO electrode under visible light. With easy chemical modification, good film-formation ability, and nice adhesion, the chitosan (CS) was modified on the NCQD/TiO2/ITO electrode to prevent the nanomaterials from peeling off from the electrode. Then the AChE was covalently immobilized on the surface of the electrode by using glutaraldehyde as the cross-linking agent. The AChE can catalytic hydrolysis of acetylcholine chloride (ATCl) to generate thiocholine which can serve as the electron donor to strengthen the separation of the photogenerated electron-hole pair, resulting in the enhanced photocurrent output. The prepared PEC biosensor was applied in the detection of OPs by using chlorpyrifos as the model. When the prepared biosensor was exposed to the solution containing chlorpyrifos, the activity of AChE was inhibited by chlorpyrifos, the obvious decrease of photocurrent response was observed. Based on the relationship between the inhibitions of chlorpyrifos and the photocurrent responses, the visible light-driven and self-powdered PEC biosensor was fabricated for rapid and sensitive detection of chlorpyrifos.

Titanium butoxide (C16H36O4Ti), Glutaraldehyde (GLD, 50% aqueous solution) and Acetylcholine chloride (ATCl) were purchased from Macklin Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ammonium citrate and ethylenediamine were obtained from Guangdong Xilong Chemical Co. Ltd. (Shantou, China). Ethanol absolute was available from Damao Chemical Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China). Acetic acid was provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Chitosan (CS) and chlorpyrifos were obtained from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Bovine serum albumin (BSA) was acquired from Bomei Biotechnology Co. Ltd. (Hefei, China). Acetylcholinesterase (AChE, Type C3389, 500 UN from electric eel) was the product of Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). ITO glass was supplied from South China Xiang Cheng Technology Co. Ltd. (Shenzhen, China, ITO coating 260 ± 2 nm, sheet resistance≤6 Ω·sq−1). All other reagents not mentioned here were of analytical grade and used as received. All aqueous solutions were prepared with ultrapure water (18 MΩ cm), which was obtained from a Milli-Q water purification system. Phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 0.1 M, pH = 7.4) prepared by mixing the stock solution of 0.2 M NaH2PO4 and 0.2 M Na2HPO4 was used as the electrolyte for the PEC measurements.

UV–vis measurements were performed on a Lambda-950 spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, American). The UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectra (DSR) were recorded from a Lambda-950 spectrometer in the range of 200–800 nm with BaSO4 as the reference. Energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) and scanning electron microscope (SEM) images were obtained from a field emission SEM (Zeiss, Germany). Fluorescence (FL) spectra were collected with a HITACHI F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi High-Tech, Japan) equipped with a 1 cm × 1 cm quartz cuvette. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were obtained by tapping-mode on Dimension Icon (Bruker). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Magna 750 FTIR spectrophotometer (Nicolet, USA) as KBr pellets. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the samples were collected with an X-ray diffractometer (D8 Advance, Germany) in the range of 10–70° (2θ). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Technai G2 F20) techniques were applied for the characterization of the NCQDs. X-ray photoelectron spectrometry (XPS) was performed on an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ESCALAB 250 Xi). The PEC tests were carried out with home-built PEC system where a 350 W Xe lamp equipped with an optical filter (Beijing Aulight technology Corporation, China, λ ≥ 420 nm) was used as the irradiation source and CHI 660E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Co. Ltd., China) was the signal detector at room temperature. The light intensity of 20 mW cm−2 was monitored by a PM200 touch screen power and energy meter (THORLABS, USA). The traditional three-electrode system was applied in all PEC measurements in which a platinum wire was used as the counter electrode, the modified ITO was serviced as the working electrode and an Ag/AgCl (3 mol L−1 KCl) was employed as the reference electrode. The Electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) was carried out with an electrochemical workstation (Zahner Zennium Pro, Germany) in 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- solution containing 0.1 M KCl at open circuit potential with the frequency varied from 0.01 Hz to 100 kHz and the amplitude was 5 mV.

TiO2 nanoparticles were prepared by the sol-gel method according to the previous report with some modification [30]. Firstly, solution A was obtained by mixing 7 mL Titanium butoxide and 30 mL anhydrous ethanol in a dry atmosphere, while solution B was prepared through mixing 20 mL anhydrous ethanol, 10 mL ultrapure water and 10 mL of acetic acid. Then, solution B was added drop by drop into solution An under stirring, the clear transparent sol was obtained. After continuous stirring for 5 h, the clear transparent sol became yellowish, which was kept for 6 h at room temperature, then it was dried at 110 °C for 8 h to acquire the TiO2 gel precursor. Subsequently, the TiO2 powder was gained by milling the xerogel in an agate mortar. The obtained powder was dispersed in 25 mL anhydrous ethanol and got the ultrasound treatment for 5 h. The suspension was centrifuged and the product was placed in the vacuum oven for 24 h at 60 °C. Finally, the TiO2 nanoparticles were obtained.

The NCQDs were prepared by the straightforward hydrothermal method according to the literature [31] with slight modification. In brief, a 1.51 g portion of ammonium citrate was added into 10 mL deionized water and the solution was stirred until the ammonium citrate completely dissolved. Then 470 mL ethylenediamine was injected into the above solution and vigorously stirred for 15 min, the obtained transparent and bright yellow solution was then transferred into 20 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and had the heat treatment for 5 h at 200 °C in an oven. After the reaction accomplished, the autoclave was cooled down to room temperature naturally. The obtained product was centrifuged for 10 min at 1000 r/min and got rid of the precipitate. The received product was the brown-black liquor and it was subjected to dialysis (MWCO = 1000) for 48 h. The rotary evaporation and the freeze-drying under vacuum condition were applied in succession to obtain the brown NCQD powder. According to the above method, the CQD was obtained by using citric acid as the carbon source without the addition of ethylenediamine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation the NCQD/TiO2 modified ITO electrode
ce:section.ce:para: Before the preparation, the ITO slices substrates (3.0 × 1.0 cm2) were sonicated in 1 M NaOH of water/acetone mixture (1:1, v/v) for 30 min, and then ITO slices were thoroughly washed with ethanol absolute and ultrapure water for three times, severally. Then the ITO slices were dried at 105 °C for 45 min.

After 6 mg TiO2 powder was dispersed in 4 mL ultrapure water and the suspension was sonicated for 2 h, 8 μL of this white suspension was drop-cast on the bare ITO electrode with the fixed area of 0.1256 cm2. The film was dried in an oven at 105 °C for 40 min, then the electrode was annealed at 450 °C for 30 min in a muffle furnace to enhance the adhesion strength between the TiO2 nanoparticles and ITO substrate. Then the TiO2/ITO electrode cooled down to the room temperature naturally. So, the stable and compact TiO2 nanoparticles modified electrode was obtained. Next, the TiO2/ITO electrode was immersed into 3 mg mL−1 NCQD solution for 10 h. Then the electrode was taken out, rinsed carefully with ultrapure water and dried at 60 °C for 30 min, successively. The desired NCQD/TiO2/ITO electrode was obtained.

The 0.08% CS solution was prepared by ultrasonically dissolving chitosan powder in a 1% acetic acid, then 8 μL of the 0.08% CS solution cast onto the NCQD/TiO2/ITO electrode and dried at 50 °C in a vacuum oven. After the electrode was carefully washed with 0.1 M NaOH and ultrapure water, respectively, 8 μL of a 5% GLD solution was added onto the electrode surface and the modified electrode was kept at room temperature for 30 min. Then, the electrode was thoroughly rinsed with DI water to remove physically adsorbed GLD. Then 8 μL of 125 U mL−1 AChE solution was introduced on the GLD/CS/NCQD/TiO2 electrode surface for 12 h at 4 °C. The AChE was bioconjugated onto the GLD/CS/NCQD/TiO2 modified ITO electrode via the coupling reaction between CHO groups on the surface of the GLD and the NH2 groups of AChE. After the AChE/GLD/CS/NCQD/TiO2 was rinsed with the PBS (0.1 M, pH = 7.4) to remove the unbound AChE, the electrode was covered with 8 μL of 0.1% BSA solution and incubated for 1 h at room temperature to block the nonspecific sites on the electrode surface. The obtained electrode was rinsed thoroughly with PBS (0.1 M, pH = 7.4) to remove the physically adsorbed BSA. Thus, the BSA/AChE/GLD/CS/NCQD/TiO2/ITO sensor electrode was prepared and it was kept at 4 °C in a refrigerator when it was not used.

For the PEC measurements, the prepared biosensor electrode was first immersed in PBS (0.1 M, pH = 7.4) containing different concentrations of standard chlorpyrifos for 8 min, and then the biosensor electrode was transferred to quartz electrochemical cell of 10 mL PBS (0.1 M, pH = 7.4) containing 0.8 mM ATCl for the measurements of the photocurrent response. An excitation light source with a wavelength of 420 nm was switched on every 10 s at without impressed voltage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The UV–vis absorption spectra of CQDs and NCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 1 A, CQDs had the strong optical absorption in the UV region and weak absorption near to the visible range. Compared with CQDs, NCQDs exhibited two characteristic peaks at around 238 nm and 340 nm respectively. The representative absorption peak at 238 nm was due to the carbon core which belonged to π-π* electronic transitions of the aromatic sp2 domains. The characteristic absorption peak at 340 nm was originated from the n–π∗ electronic transition of CO and CN bond, which was attributed to the formation of excited defect surface state caused by nitrogen-doping (N heteroatoms) [32]. In other words, the nitrogen-doping made the surface groups of NCQDs more multitudinous. Under room light, the transparent solutions of CQD and NCQD exhibited no fluorescence (inset left). Under UV excitation from 365 nm lamp, the CQD solution showed low intense fluorescence, however, the NCQD solution exhibited the intense blue fluorescence (inset right), that was possibly attributed to their small size effect and surface state effect [33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The PL spectrum of CQDs and NCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1B and E show the PL spectra of CQDs and NCQDs. CQDs and NCQDs exhibited excitation wavelength-dependent phenomenon. It was obvious that the emission peak of CQDs shifted from 406 nm to 483 nm and the emission peak of NCQDs changed from 437 nm to 500 nm when the excitation wavelength varied from 300 nm to 420 nm in 10 nm increments (Fig. 1C and F), besides that, the emission peak intensity increased firstly and then decreases for them. As shown in Fig. 1B and E, the optimal excitation wavelength of the CQDs was 330 nm and the maximum emission wavelength was 417 nm, showing the stokes shift of 87 nm. Analogously, the optimal excitation wavelength of the NCQDs was 370 nm and the maximum emission wavelength was 447 nm, showing the stokes shift of 77 nm.

The excitation wavelength-dependent properties may be owned to the distribution of the different particle sizes and surface energy trap of the obtained CQDs and NCQDs [34]. The energy gap (Eg) was estimated at 2.97 eV for CQDs and 2.77 eV for NCQDs that obtained from the PL peak locked at 417 nm and 447 nm, respectively. As shown in Fig. S1 and Table S1, the quantum yield (QY) of CQDs and NCQDs were 6.27% and 84.73%, respectively.

Obviously, as shown in Fig. 1D and G, the as-gained CQDs and NCQDs emerged the clear upconversion PL property, that was derived from the multiphoton active process, in which the CQDs or NCQDs can absorb two or more photons simultaneously and then resulted in the emission at a shorter wavelength than the excitation wavelength (anti-Stokes type emission) [35]. This up-conversion PL property can be attributed to the electronic anti-Stokes transition from the energy levels of π to σ, which was originated from the carbene ground-state multiplicity. When some low energy photons, such as excitation light with wavelength (λ > 600 nm), was used to excite the electrons of the π orbitals, the electrons can transfer to a higher energy state (LUMO), and then the up-converted emission generated as the electrons went back to the ground-σ state [36].

Interestingly, the optimal upconversion excitation wavelengths of CQDs and NCQDs were both 780 nm, nevertheless, the maximum upconversion emission wavelengths are 463 nm for CQDs accompanying with the anti-stokes shift of 317 nm and 446 nm for NCQDs accompanying with the anti-stokes shift of 334 nm. Nevertheless, when the excitation wavelengths were at 500 nm, 520 nm, 540 nm, 560 nm and 580 nm, no detectable signals of CQDs can be obtained. So, there was no doubt that the NCQDs had the better ability to absorb long wavelength light and then emitted shorter wavelength light.
Based on above analyses results, it was known that NCQDs had the narrower band energy and superior upconversion effect. Hence, NCQDs were selected for the further characterization and experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The characterizations of TiO2 nanoparticles, NCQDs and NCQD/TiO2 nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: SEM images and the size distribution (inset) of TiO2 are shown in Fig. 1H, TiO2 nanoparticles were quasi-spherical with the size range of 11.61–45.84 nm, giving the average size of 27.19 nm. The TEM images and the size distribution (inset) of NCQDs are shown in Fig. 1I, revealing that NCQDs all were uniform distribution and approximately 1.47–3.95 nm in size and the most probable diameter of 2.69 nm. The AFM image of the obtained NCQDs (Fig. 2 A) reveals that the height of these particles was about 0.98–3.86 nm, it was in agreement with the TEM characterization.

XRD measurement was performed to detect the crystal structure of the prepared NCQDs and TiO2 nanoparticles. As exhibited in Fig. 2B, NCQDs (curve b) shows a broad peak at 2θ = 24°, indicating the evidence of highly disordered carbon atoms [37]. The diffraction peaks of TiO2 (curve c) at 2θ = 25.29°, 37.90°, 48.10°, 54.07°, 54.93°, 62.76° and 70.35° corresponded to (101), (004), (200), (105), (211), (204) and (220) lattice planes of anatase (TiO2) [JCPDS 01-089-4921]; Interestingly, no obvious diffraction single for NCQDs was detected in the NCQD/TiO2 (curve a), because the NCQD/TiO2 nanocomposites had the low levels of NCQDs and the crystal of TiO2 nanoparticles was dominated.

The EDX spectrum (Fig. 2C) and elemental mapping images (Fig. 2D) provided the other evidence to prove that the as-prepared NCQDs product major contained C, N, O elements, and these elements dispersed uniformly. The FTIR (Fig. S1B) and XPS spectra (Figs. S1C–F) of NCQDs were provided in the Supporting Information. All results proved that the NCQDs were successfully prepared.

As shown in Fig. 2E, the UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra were obtained to understand the optical properties of the as-gained TiO2 and NCQD/TiO2. Compared with pure TiO2, the NCQD/TiO2 not only had the obvious absorption in UV light region but also showed the enhanced absorption in visible light region. The band gap values obtained from the tangents to plots (Fig. 2F) (αhν)1/2 versus photon energy (hν) of the TiO2 and NCQD/TiO2 were calculated to be about 3.20 eV and 2.94 eV, respectively. This suggested that the NCQD/TiO2 nanocomposites can take the full use of the visible light during the PEC biosensor process.

Fig. 2G exhibits the linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves of TiO2, NCQD/TiO2 under dark and NCQD/TiO2 under visible-light irradiation in 0.1 M Na2SO4. The LSV result indicated that the pure TiO2/ITO photoelectrode (curve a) showed small photocurrent under visible-light irradiation in the potential range from −0.1 V to 0.7 V vs. Ag/AgCl. That was attributed to the high recombination rate of the photogenerated electron-hole pair. However, the NCQD/TiO2/ITO photoelectrode (curve c) exhibited obviously increased photocurrent intensity compared with TiO2 photoelectrode under the same test conditions. Furthermore, the NCQD/TiO2/ITO photoelectrode (curve b) displayed the higher photocurrent under visible-light irradiation than dark. The results were owed to the broad light absorption range, the high photon-to-electron-conversion efficiency and fast charge separation of the NCQD/TiO2/ITO electrode.

The open circuit potential (OCP) tests were carried out under dark and visible-light irradiation to verify the PEC performance of NCQD/TiO2 complex. As depicted in Fig. 2H. OCP of the PEC cell represented the difference in Fermi level between the photoactive materials (NCQD/TiO2) and counter electrodes. In the dark, the electrode potential was dictated by redox equilibration. Under light illumination, the majority of the photogenerated electrons were accumulated in the NCQD/TiO2 films, which resulted in a negative shift of the Fermi level and caused the sudden increase in OCP. At the same time, the recombination of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs occurred. Then the OCP can arrive at the steady state. Hence, the changes of OCP can indicate the charge separation efficiency of the TiO2 and NCQD/TiO2. When the irradiation stopped, the OCP declined, owing to the high recombination of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs and the scavenging of the electrons by electron acceptor substances in solution. Compared with TiO2 (curve a), the higher decrease OCP of NCQD/TiO2 (curve b) under light illumination indicated that more photogenerated electrons were collected on the surface of the NCQD/TiO2, suggesting the more efficient separation and transition of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs. These all revealed that NCQDs can successfully sensitize TiO2.

The EIS date (Fig. 2I) of TiO2/ITO and NCQD/TiO2/ITO electrodes under illumination were also recorded. Compared with the Ret values under the dark, the Ret values of both TiO2/ITO (curve b') and NCQD/TiO2/ITO electrode (curve a') were smaller under the illumination respectively, which can be due to the photo-electrical effect of the photoactive semiconductor materials [38]. In addition, under illumination conditions, the Ret value of NCQD/TiO2/ITO (curve a') was smaller than that of TiO2/ITO (curve b'), therefore, the results proved that the NCQD/TiO2/ITO electrode held the good electrical conductivity, excellent charge separation, and transition capacity once more.
The above results indicated that the NCQD/TiO2/ITO electrode showed the improved utilization of visible light and the electron transfer capability.

EIS is a valuable and effective tool to characterize surface modifications and monitor the fabrication process of the biosensor. Fig. 3 A shows the Nyquist plots of EIS of the different step modified electrodes. These EIS data were fitted to a general electronic equivalent circuit (inset of Fig. 3A) [39], which contains the electrolyte resistance (Rs), the double-layer capacitance (Cdl), the charge transfer resistance (Ret) and the Warburg diffusion impedance (Zw). In a typical EIS spectrum, the semicircle diameter of EIS represented the electron transfer resistance (Ret), which depended on the electron transfer kinetics of the redox probe at the electrode interface. The bare ITO electrode exhibited the small semicircle with the Ret value of 34 Ω (curve a). After TiO2 was drop-cast onto the surface of the ITO electrode, the Ret value of the TiO2/ITO electrode got bigger (62 Ω), indicating that the compact TiO2 film hindered the access of the redox probe to the electrode surface. Subsequently, the TiO2/ITO electrode was modified with NCQDs, the NCQD/TiO2/ITO electrode showed a smaller semicircle and Ret value was about 54 Ω (curve c), revealing that the NCQDs can achieve the more efficient interfacial electron transfer. While CS, GLD, AChE, and BSA were introduced on the electrode step by step. Ret of each related electrode increased correspondingly (curves d-g) because the hindrance effect and insulating effect of them obstructed the diffusion of the redox probe to the electrode surface. From the Ret changes of the corresponding electrode, the result suggested that the proposed biosensor was fabricated successfully.

Meanwhile, the photocurrent responses of the various modified electrodes (Fig. 3B) were recorded. Compared with the bare ITO electrode (curve a), the TiO2/ITO electrode (curve b) showed the slight photocurrent, indicating the low utilization of the visible light. The photocurrent of NCQDs/TiO2/ITO electrode (curve c) was about 42 times than that of the TiO2/ITO electrode, it suggested that the NCQDs showed the efficient sensitization effect on TiO2. Then the CS, GLD, AChE, and BSA were introduced on the NCQD/TiO2/ITO electrode in sequence, the photocurrent decreased gradually (curve d-g), suggesting that the insulating and steric hindrance effect of them impeded the transfer of the electrons between the electrode and electrolyte. When the obtained BSA/AChE/GLD/CS/NCQD/TiO2/ITO electrode immersed in PBS (0.1 M, pH = 7.4) containing 0.8 mM ATCl, the photocurrent (curve f) exhibited the clear increase, this was attributed to that the generated thiocholine acted as the electron donor to enhance the dissociation of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves and SEM images of the different step modified electrodes are displayed in Fig. S2, respectively. The details of the discussion were provided in the Supporting Information. As mentioned above, all results demonstrated the successful modification process of the PEC biosensor.

To obtain the best performance for PEC detection of chlorpyrifos, the experimental conditions had been optimized. According to Fig. 4 , the optimal experimental conditions were as follows: (A) TiO2 concentration of 1.5 mg mL−1, (B) the TiO2/ITO electrodes immersion time of 10 h, (C) the AChE concentration of 125 U mL−1, (D) the AChE incubation time of 10 h, (E) ATCl concentration of 0.8 mM, (F) solution pH of 7.4, (G) the inhibition time of 8 min. The details were provided in the Supporting Information.

Under the above optimal experimental conditions, the as-developed PEC biosensor was employed to monitor the various concentrations of chlorpyrifos. Conspicuously, when the chlorpyrifos concentrations increased, the photocurrent of the obtained PEC biosensor lowered continuously (Fig. 5 A and B). The nice linear relationship between the photocurrent signal of the PEC biosensor and the logarithm of chlorpyrifos concentration was acquired. The linear regression equation was I (nA) = 336.63–84.19 logC (μg mL−1) (R2 = 0.9947) with chlorpyrifos concentration in the range from the 0.001 μg mL−1 to 1.5 μg mL−1, and the detection limit was about 0.07 ng mL−1 (S/N = 3). As listed in Table S2, compared with some previous reports, the detection range of the prepared PEC biosensor was broader and the detection limit was lower. The result demonstrated that the as-fabricated PEC biosensor was qualified for the sensitive detection of chlorpyrifos.

The outstanding performance of the PEC biosensor owed to three parts as follows: i) on account of the splendid absorption capacity in the visible region and preeminent upconversion light character in the long wavelength range of NCQDs, after NCQDs coupled with TiO2, the visible light utilization of NCQD/TiO2 enhanced significantly, the output of photocurrent signal amplified hugely; ii) endowed with the satisfactory electrical conductivity, NCQDs can strengthen the electron transmission and decelerated the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. iii) the thiocholine, the catalytic product of ATCl, served as the electron donor to strengthen the separation of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs and heightened the photocurrent signal.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reproducibility, stability and selectivity of PEC biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The reproducibility (Fig. 5C) of the obtained PEC biosensor was tested by the inter-assay where seven as-prepared biosensor electrodes were applied to monitor 0.04 μg mL−1 chlorpyrifos, and relative standard deviation (RSD) was about 2.02%, the result was acceptable.

The stability (Fig. 5D) of the PEC biosensor was also studied. The PEC biosensor electrodes which were fabricated with the same process were stored at 4 °C for different times. Compared with the original intensity of the photocurrent signal, after the electrodes had stored for a week, the photocurrents reduced about 3.98%, when the storage time reached to two weeks, the photocurrent declined about 8.93%, these results substantiated that the AChE on the electrode still had catalytic activity. So, that result proved that the as-prepared PEC biosensor exhibited the receivable stability.

The selectivity (Fig. 5E) of the as-gained PEC biosensor was also explored. The photocurrent intensities of the as-prepared PEC biosensor electrodes were obtained in PBS (0.1 M, pH = 7.4) containing 0.8 mM ATCl in the presence of 1 μg ml−1 chlorpyrifos, 1 μg ml−1 other pesticides (δ-Hexachlorocyclohexane, Fenvalerate, Deltamethrin, 2,2-Bis(4-chlorophenyl)-1,1-dichloroethylene (p, p’-DDE), 2,4′-Dichlorodiphenyltrichloroethane (o,p′-DDT)), and 100 μg ml−1 inorganic ion (K+, Na+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Cl−, NO3 −, SO4 2−). It was clear that no distinct changes of photocurrent intensities were appeared except in the presence of chlorpyrifos. This result highlighted that the as-gained PEC biosensor had a satisfactory selectivity.

To explore the practical application of the PEC biosensor, the PEC biosensor was utilized to detect chlorpyrifos in lake water and Chinese cabbage accompanied with the standard addition method. The 10 mL of the lake water sample was centrifuged at 8000 r/min for 10 min, then the supernatant was taken out and diluted with PBS (0.1 M, pH = 7.4) to 45 mL. For the preparation of Chinese cabbage sample, 5 g Chinese cabbage was Chopped into small pieces and put in the centrifuge tube, 5 mL acetonitrile was added into the centrifuge tube and then the centrifuge tube was shocked for 30 min and centrifuged at 8000 r/min for 10 min. The supernatant was diluted with PBS (0.1 M, pH = 7.4) to 45 mL. Then the known concentrations of chlorpyrifos solutions were added to the prepared samples solution for recovery tests under optimal experimental conditions. As exhibited in Table 1 , the recovery values of these experiments changed from 95.0% to 101.3%, severally. These results proved that the as-prepared PEC biosensor can be applied for OPs in real samples.

From the PL results of NCQDs, the PL peak located at 447 nm, so the Eg of the NCQDs can be calculated at 2.77 eV. In addition, based on the reported result [40], the work function (ϕ) of CQDs was near 4.0 eV which was measured from ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS). Therefore, the conduction band (CB) energy (EC) of the NCQDs can be calculated to be −0.5 eV based on normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) at −4.5 eV. According to the formula EV = EC + Eg, the valence band (VB) energy (EV) of the NCQDs was estimated to be 2.27 eV. As depicted in Fig. 6 A, the CB of the NCQDs was more negative than that of TiO2, and the VB of TiO2 was positive than that of the NCQDs. So, the energy levels of the NCQDs and TiO2 matched well. Under illumination, the photogenerated electrons can quickly transfer from the CB of the NCQDs to the CB of TiO2, the electrons on the CB of TiO2 not only can directly shift to the ITO but also can first migrate to the NCQDs and then move to ITO, while the holes on the VB of TiO2 can transfer to the VB of the NCQDs. So, the photocurrent signal was generated. Based on the up-conversion PL property of the NCQDs (Fig. 1G), when the excitation wavelength was larger than 500 nm, the emission wavelength of NCQDs ranged from 442 nm to 505 nm. From the UV–vis DRS results of TiO2 (Fig. 2E) and the NCQD/TiO2 nanocomposite (Fig. 2F), pure TiO2 merely had the absorptions in UV light region, however, the NCQD/TiO2 nanocomposite not only had the distinct absorptions in UV light region but also revealed the absorption in the visible light region. Hence, the other possible mechanism of the as-prepared PEC biosensor for chlorpyrifos was proposed. As shown in Fig. 6B, under illumination, the production of photogenerated electron-hole pairs occurred on both the NCQD/TiO2 nanocomposites and the outermost NCQDs, then the photogenerated electron-hole pairs separated, and the electrons on the CB of the NCQDs transferred to the CB of the NCQD/TiO2 nanocomposites, then the electrons shifted to the ITO. The holes on the VB of NCQD/TiO2 nanocomposites migrated to the VB of the NCQDs. During this process, the NCQDs can absorb long wavelength light (λ ≥ 500 nm) and emission short wavelength light (442 nm≤λ ≤ 505 nm) which can be utilized by the NCQD/TiO2 nanocomposites. In this way, the utilization of visible light was improved. In the two possible mechanisms, the thiocholine, enzyme-catalyzed product of ATCl, can save as the electron donor for scavenging the holes to promote the separation photogenerated electron-hole pairs. In the presence of chlorpyrifos, the catalytic activity of AChE was inhibited, so, the output of electron donor declines, leading to the reduction of the photocurrent signal. Therefore, according to the relationship between the photocurrent intensity of the PEC biosensor and the concentration of the chlorpyrifos, the as-fabricated PEC biosensor can achieve the fast and sensitive detection of chlorpyrifos.

In summary, the elaborated visible light-driven and self-powdered PEC biosensor was prepared for OPs detection. With the excellent optical performance and the remarkable electrical conductivity, NCQDs exhibited excellent sensitization performance on the NCQD/TiO2/ITO electrode. Based on the enzyme inhibition, the prepared PEC biosensor can achieve the sensitive detection of OPs by using chlorpyrifos as the model. The obtained PEC biosensor revealed the broad detection range and low detection limit. In addition, this PEC biosensor demonstrated the acceptable reproducibility, stability, and selectivity. For the real samples (lake water and the Chinese cabbage) detection, the satisfactory recovery values were obtained from this PEC biosensor. Hence, the as-prepared visible light driven self-powdered PEC biosensor can offer the new route for OPs monitoring in environmental and food.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful for the financial support from the Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province (No. 2014A030313480) and the Science & Technology Project of Guangdong Province (No. 2013B030600001 & No. 2016A020223014).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2018.11.086.
